title: Grain direction in paper
subtitle: What paper grain is, how to test it, and why it matters for bookbinding and folding
excerpt: Machine-made paper has a grain direction set by the alignment of fibres during manufacture. Grain affects how paper folds, tears, warps, and ages in a binding. Long-grain paper has the grain parallel to the long edge; short-grain paper has it parallel to the short edge.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on paper and its grain. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell gives the fold-and-flex test and the consequence of mis-grained paper in a binding.

glossaryTerms.definition: The predominant alignment of fibres in a sheet of machine-made paper, set during manufacture as the pulp flows along the moving wire. Paper folds more crisply parallel to the grain and more stiffly across it.
Paper in which the grain direction runs parallel to the long edge of the sheet. An A4 long-grain sheet has the grain running top to bottom (the 297 mm dimension).
Paper in which the grain direction runs parallel to the short edge of the sheet. An A4 short-grain sheet has the grain running left to right (the 210 mm dimension).

content.text: When paper pulp flows across the moving wire of a Fourdrinier machine, the cellulose fibres align with the direction of travel. The result is a sheet where the fibres run predominantly in one direction. This is the

, and it affects almost every use of the sheet: how it folds, how it absorbs moisture, how it warps in a binding, and how far it tears cleanly. Handmade paper has no grain in the same sense: the fibres settle randomly as the sheet forms on the mould, so handmade paper behaves roughly the same in both directions.
Three tests, each taking about ten seconds:

Bend test: hold a sheet horizontally and bend it by arching the top and bottom edges toward each other, without creasing. Note the resistance. Rotate the sheet 90 degrees and repeat. The sheet will flex more readily in the direction parallel to the grain than across it.

Tear test: tear a short distance from the edge. Tear parallel to what you suspect is the grain, then across it. The parallel-to-grain tear is cleaner and more nearly straight. The cross-grain tear wanders and leaves a rougher edge.

Moisture test: moisten one side of a narrow strip of the paper with a damp sponge. The strip will curl. The curl runs parallel to the grain: the sheet curls across the grain because fibres swell preferentially in the transverse direction. The direction the curl rolls toward is the grain direction.
means the grain runs the 297 mm dimension (top to bottom when the sheet is in portrait orientation).

means the grain runs the 210 mm dimension (left to right in portrait). Commercial printing paper is typically sold as long-grain A4 or short-grain A4; the designation is sometimes marked on the paper ream wrapper.
Why grain direction matters for bookbinding

The grain must run parallel to the spine in a bound book. A book that is bound with the grain running across the spine (cross-grain) will fight the binding every time the cover opens. The pages resist turning. Moisture in the air swells the cross-grain fibres, causing the boards to warp. Cockerell observed that the most common cause of early binding failure in home-made books was cross-grained paper in the cover boards.

For a standard portrait A5 book bound at the long edge, the spine is the 210 mm dimension. The grain should also run the 210 mm dimension of the signature sheets: long-grain A5 (cut from long-grain A4 by folding along the 297 mm axis gives a short edge of 148 mm at the foredge and 210 mm at head and tail). Any cover boards must have the same grain direction as the text block.
Grain direction in origami and folded structures

For origami, grain direction affects how crisply a crease forms. Folding with the grain gives a sharper fold. Folding across the grain can crack the surface of heavier papers. In a complex model, the first folds should run with the grain where possible. Purpose-made origami paper is lightly grained and lightweight, which reduces the effect.
